comment_text.sn2.13:1.3: Dīghalaṭṭhi (“long stick”) is otherwise unknown. Following the previous Sutta, perhaps to be traced to Vishnu in the sense “wide success” (dīrghavyaṣṭi) in reference to his broad strides (cp. Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 13.5.4.11).

root_text.sn2.13:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā rājagahe viharati veḷuvane kalandakanivāpe.
root_text.sn2.13:1.3: Atha kho dīghalaṭṭhi devaputto abhikkantāya rattiyā abhikkantavaṇṇo kevalakappaṁ veḷuvanaṁ obhāsetvā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ aṭṭhāsi.
root_text.sn2.13:1.4: Ekamantaṁ ṭhito kho dīghalaṭṭhi devaputto bhagavato santike imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:

translation_text.sn2.13:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground.
translation_text.sn2.13:1.3: Then, late at night, the glorious godling Dīghalaṭṭhi, lighting up the entire Bamboo Grove, went up to the Buddha, bowed, stood to one side,
translation_text.sn2.13:1.4: and spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence:
translation_text.sn2.13:2.1: “If a mendicant is a meditator, freed in mind,
translation_text.sn2.13:2.2: and if they desire the heart’s fulfillment—
translation_text.sn2.13:2.3: having known the arising and passing of the world,
translation_text.sn2.13:2.4: good-hearted, independent, that is their reward.”
